does: This paper introduces POLARIS, a framework that systematically generates safety tests for large language models by formalizing natural-language safety policies into First-Order Logic representations. The core contribution is converting unstructured policy specifications into a Semantic Policy Graph where nodes and edges represent policy constraints and their violations. By systematically traversing this graph, the framework automatically discovers compositional violation patterns—scenarios where multiple policy violations combine—and instantiates these patterns into concrete natural-language test cases. This approach moves beyond ad-hoc red-teaming by providing structured, reproducible safety evaluation grounded in explicit policy specifications rather than expert intuition.
connects: This work builds on two established paradigms in LLM safety evaluation: static benchmark-based testing and dynamic red-teaming approaches. It extends formal methods from software testing, particularly specification-based testing frameworks that have long used logical formalizations to derive comprehensive test suites. The paper responds to limitations in existing safety evaluation—their dependence on expert knowledge, lack of systematic coverage guarantees, and vulnerability to model evolution—by importing verification techniques from formal verification and model checking literature. It combines insights from policy analysis, formal semantics, and automated test generation to create a more principled alternative to current safety evaluation practices.
enables: This framework enables several downstream capabilities: automated discovery of novel safety vulnerabilities through systematic policy graph exploration, reproducible and transparent safety audits traceable to explicit policy specifications, and continuous safety evaluation as policies evolve. The formalization of natural-language policies into FOL creates infrastructure for compositional safety analysis, potentially enabling cost-effective scaling of safety testing without constant expert intervention. It points toward verification-centric approaches for AI safety where policy compliance can be assessed systematically rather than opportunistically, and opens possibilities for comparing safety across models using standardized policy specifications, ultimately supporting more rigorous governance of LLM deployment in high-stakes domains.
